text: The surgeon enters the patient's abdomen using an optical entry technique through the right upper quadrant.
The surgeon considers making a high incision in the right upper quadrant of the abdomen.
The surgeon places a port in the right upper quadrant of the patient's abdomen, preparing for optical entry.
The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum, inflating the abdominal cavity to create space for the procedure.
The surgeon places the staple port approximately halfway between the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) and the umbilicus, in the abdominal region.
The surgeon identifies the location for the camera port placement, aligning it with the previously determined trajectory and anatomical landmarks.
The surgeon visually maps the trajectory of the ports, placing the next port eight to nine centimeters from the previous one, with the camera port positioned at the designated location.
The surgeon measures two to three centimeters to the left of the falciform ligament to configure the second port, stepping off from the anterior superior iliac spine (ASI).

The surgeon places robotic ports along a previously drawn yellow line, starting with the state report in the right lower quadrant. The camera port is positioned 8 to 9 centimeters above the state report, followed by the number two arm and number three arm. Assist ports are located in the right upper corner.

The surgeon uses laparoscopy to view the right lower quadrant of the insufflated abdomen, measuring the distance between the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) and the umbilicus to determine the placement of the staple port.
The surgeon positions the camera port approximately eight to nine centimeters to the left of the previously measured staple port location, just above and to the right of the umbilicus.
The surgeon draws a straight line laterally, extending almost to the left paracolic gutter, positioned just anterior to the white line of Toldt.
The surgeon positions the number three robotic arm at the predetermined location, following precise measurements for optimal placement.
